# Should COMP110 Add Lesson Videos?

<!-- This is a comment. Below, you'll see code for inserting an image. To make this image appear, update <custom-path>. To add an image, save it inside the imgs folder of this repository. -->
<img src="static/imgs/logo.png" alt="Image of Comp110 rainbow logo. "  width="500"/>

## Problem Overview

Have you ever missed a class and struggled to catch up afterward? Or have you ever learned a complicated concept in class but wished you had some way to hear the professor explain it again?

When we were in COMP110, we noticed that it was difficult to learn and understand class material when classes were missed due to illness or other circumstances. There were also occassions in which hearing the professor explain the topic again could have helped us understand the context behind topics. 

As a result, we wanted to investigate whether it would be beneficial for other COMP110 students to add lecture recordings and lesson videos by analyzing data from a class survey. We also wanted to see if students were interested in these additions.

## Potential Benefits of Lesson Videos:

*Helps students review any missed or misunderstood concepts from lecture. 
*Lecture recordings provide concepts in depth and professor explanations.
*Beneficial for students who may have missed class.

## Analysis of Data and Graphs

To see if having lecture videos would be helpful for learning, we compared percieved class difficulty ranking to wanting livestreams and believing that having lesson videos would help learning. We also considered the number of responses each rating had and their percentage of the total.

Graph 1: 
img src="static/imgs/add_livestream vs difficulty Line Graph.png" alt="add_livestream vs difficulty Line Graph."  width="500"

This line graph shows the relationship between how difficult the students percieved the class and how much they would prefer adding a live streamed lecture (with 1 being strongly disagree, and 7 being strongly agree). 

What this showed: Students who found the course to be more difficult tended to want a live stream option more. This possibly suggests having a live stream of the class may result in the class feeling less difficult, which is a great benefit to students! Having students succeed in class is very important. 


Graph 2: 
img src="static/imgs/ls_effective vs difficulty Line Graph.png" alt="ls_effective vs difficulty Line Graph."  width="500"

This line graph shows the relationship between how difficult the students percieved the class and how much they think lesson videos help them learn course concepts (with 1 being strongly disagree, and 7 being strongly agree). 

What this showed: Students who found the course to be more difficult tended to say that having lesson videos would help them learn. Since the class did not have lecture videos, adding lesson videos may help many students learn course concepts more easily. 


Graph 3: 
img src="static/imgs/ls_effective Bar Graph.png" alt="ls_effective Bar Graph."  width="500"

This bar graph shows the frequency of each student response. Responses were a number from 1-7 that represent how much students agreed that lesson videos would be helpful class material (with 1 being strongly disagree, and 7 being strongly agree). 

What this showed: Most students agreed with the idea that lesson videos would be helpful, showing that most students will likely benefit from the addition of videos that cover lecture material, like lecture recordings.


Graph 4: 
img src="static/imgs/add_livestream Bar Graph.png" alt="add_livestream Bar Graph."  width="500"

This bar graph shows the frequency of each student response. Responses were a number from 1-7 that represent how much students would want a livestream option (with 1 being strongly disagree, and 7 being strongly agree).

What this showed: A majority of students support a live stream option. This shows that students would likely want and use a live stream option, and suggests that it could be helpful for student learning.


Percentage Data:

Percentage of total each response made up for ls_effective: 
'1': 1.68 %
'2': 2.62 %
'3': 8.05 %
'4': 20.5 %
'5': 22.4 %
'6': 19.1 %
'7': 25.4 %

Percentage of total each response made up for add_livestream:
'1': 1.68 %
'2': 3.74 %
'3': 6.92 %
'4': 14.0 %
'5': 19.6 %
'6': 16.2 %
'7': 37.6 %

What this showed: For both variables, '7', which means 'strongly agree' was the largest percentage of the total responses. Additionally, for both, the percentage of people who answered 'slightly agree', 'agree', or 'strongly agree' was much larger than the percentage who did not support adding livestreams or believe that lesson videos would not help their learning. This suggests that a majority of students want to add a livestream, and that a majority of students would benefit from them. 


## Final Takeaways and Considerations

Based on the data, our idea that adding lesson recordings to COMP110 will be beneficial to students is strongly supported. 

Because a large majority of students seem to want and will benefit from having lesson videos, and class difficulty may decrease if lesson videos are added to the course, we suggest that an addition of recordings and videos will improve the course overall and make students more succsessful. 

However, improving the proposed idea and considering drawbacks is always important when considering course changes. 

To explore this idea further, we could:
*Create a new survey that asks what type of lesson videos students would prefer
*See if students would prefer to just have livestreamed lectures, or if they prefer having access to livestreams even after class ends. 
*After trying it for one semester, we could do another survey assessing whether or not students used the lesson videos and how helpful they thought they were. 
*We could also look at quiz scores from previous semesters to see if scores improved when lesson videos were added. 

Potential trade offs:
*Students' attendance may decrease if video lectures are available, making it easier for them to skip class. *Students may also pay attention less in class due to the false security of these lecture videos. 
*Recording lectures may also require additional technology, storage, and preparation time for professors.
